key_concepts: Embeddings require efficient storage and comparison mechanisms to be useful in production.
Feature banks (or vector databases) are used to index and retrieve high-dimensional vectors quickly.
Similarity metrics define how the distance or relationship between two embedding vectors is calculated.
Cosine similarity is the most common metric for embeddings as it measures the angle between vectors, ignoring their magnitude.
Dot product is a computationally cheaper alternative that is identical to cosine similarity if the vectors are L2-normalized.
Euclidean distance measures the straight-line distance between points, making it sensitive to vector magnitude.

definitions.definition: A specialized storage system optimized for indexing, storing, and querying high-dimensional vectors.
A metric that measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, indicating their directional alignment.

examples: Querying a feature bank of product embeddings to find the most similar items to a user's recent purchase using cosine similarity.

relations.precedes: ch15_s03 (evaluating if these embeddings and similarities actually make sense).
relations.follows: ch15_s01 (generating embeddings via extraction or fine-tuning).

exclusions: Do not delve into the algorithmic details of approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search like HNSW or KD-trees.
